f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.name: AWAF evasion-technique explainer
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.blurb: Type a sub-violation name or "evasions" for F5's eight evasion sub-violations explained, each with its default and the encoding trick it catches, or paste the evasions block of a declarative WAF policy to read each one back as enabled or disabled with the Multiple-decoding pass count. Grounded in F5 K7929; runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.inputLabel: A sub-violation name, "evasions", or a policy's evasions block
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.inputPlaceholder:
Type "evasions" for all eight, or a name like "Multiple decoding", or paste:
{ "blocking-settings": { "evasions": [ { "description": "Multiple decoding", "enabled": true, "maxDecodingPasses": 3 } ] } }
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.empty: Type "evasions" for F5's eight sub-violations, a single name, or paste an evasions block to read it back.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.nameMiss: That is not one of F5's eight evasion sub-violations. Try "evasions" for the full list.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.passesDefault: Multiple-decoding passes: not set, so the default of {value} applies.
f5-awaf-evasion-explainer.bridgeNote: Several of these are the same decode operations this toolbox performs: %u, bare-byte and the %XX in Bad unescape are the Base64/Percent codec's territory, and Multiple decoding is repeated percent-decoding. The related tool on each card lets you see the decode by hand.

notes.notName: That is not JSON and not a known sub-violation name.
notes.parseError: That looks like JSON but could not be parsed
notes.noBlock: This JSON has no evasions array. Paste the evasions block, or a policy whose blocking-settings contains one.
notes.allDefault: No evasion sub-violation was explicitly set here, so every one takes its template default (all eight are Enabled by default).
notes.disabled: Disabled here: {names}. A disabled sub-violation means that normalization or check is skipped, so its evasion trick can slip past the signatures.
notes.passesRange: Multiple-decoding passes is {value}, outside the schema's 2-to-5 range. The system would reject or clamp this value.
notes.passesRaised: Multiple-decoding passes is raised to {value} (default is 3). More passes catch deeper nested encoding but cost slightly more processing.
notes.unknownEntry: An evasions entry has an unrecognized description ({description}); it is not one of F5's eight sub-violations.
notes.learnGated: Learning is disabled on the parent "Evasion technique detected" violation, so per the schema these sub-violations are not learned even when a request triggers them.

pct-u.name: %u decoding
pct-u.what: Performs Microsoft %u-style Unicode decoding on the URI and parameters, so a character written as %u00XX is resolved to what it really is before the signatures see it.
pct-u.catches: Attacks hidden behind Microsoft %u Unicode escapes, a classic way to write a metacharacter so a naive filter misses it.

apache-ws.name: Apache whitespace
apache-ws.what: Detects the whitespace bytes ASCII 9, 11, 12 and 13 in the URI, control characters some servers treat as separators.
apache-ws.catches: Requests that smuggle payloads past a filter using unusual whitespace that the origin server tolerates.

bad-unescape.name: Bad unescape
bad-unescape.what: Detects illegal hex encoding, a percent sign not followed by two valid hex digits (for example %RR).
bad-unescape.catches: Malformed percent-escapes crafted so the WAF and the server disagree on what the sequence decodes to.

bare-byte.name: Bare byte decoding
bare-byte.what: Detects higher-ASCII bytes, raw values above 127 sent unescaped in the request.
bare-byte.catches: Non-ASCII bytes injected directly to represent characters a signature is watching for, without the encoding that would reveal them.

dir-traversal.name: Directory traversals
dir-traversal.what: Detects path-traversal patterns such as ../ that walk up the directory tree.
dir-traversal.catches: Attempts to escape the intended path and reach files elsewhere on the server, the core of a path-traversal attack.

iis-backslash.name: IIS backslashes
iis-backslash.what: Normalizes the backslash \ to a forward slash /, the way IIS treats the two as equivalent in a path.
iis-backslash.catches: Payloads that use \ where the signatures expect /, relying on the server to fold them together after the WAF has looked.

iis-unicode.name: IIS Unicode codepoints
iis-unicode.what: Decodes IIS-specific %u code-point mappings from the Windows-1252 code page.
iis-unicode.catches: The IIS Unicode-escape evasions that famously let attackers write traversal and metacharacters in a form only IIS would decode.

multi-decode.name: Multiple decoding
multi-decode.what: Decodes the URI and parameter values repeatedly, up to the configured number of passes, so payloads hidden under several layers of encoding are unwrapped before inspection.
multi-decode.catches: Double- and triple-encoded payloads, where a value is percent-encoded more than once so a single-pass decoder never reveals the real characters.

f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.name: AWAF automatic-learning poisoning estimator
f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.blurb: How many requests does an attacker need to drill a hole through your BIG-IP Advanced WAF policy when the Policy Builder is left in Automatic learning against untrusted traffic? Enter your Loosen thresholds and the attacker's resources; it computes the minimum sources, requests, and time to force one automatic relaxation, and gates on the rules that make it impossible. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented Policy Builder behaviour, not a probe. It never contacts a BIG-IP. Read your policy's real Loosen thresholds from Security > Application Security > Policy Building > Learning and Blocking Settings.

f.attackerTrusted: Attacker's IPs are in the Trusted IP list
f.ratingFactorHint: F5 requires more for higher ratings but does not publish the curve; set your own (1 = model only the threshold)

verdict.learning-disabled: Safe: learning is Disabled — the policy never changes itself.
verdict.manual: Safe: Manual mode — a human must accept every relaxation, so there is no automatic hole to drill.
verdict.unlearnable: Safe: a rating-5 violation is unlearnable — the Policy Builder never auto-relaxes it, no matter how much traffic arrives.
verdict.trusted-only-blocked: Safe: loosening is restricted to trusted traffic, so an untrusted attacker cannot reach the Loosen path.
verdict.insufficient-sources: Out of reach: the attacker does not control enough distinct sources to meet the Loosen threshold.
verdict.drillable: Drillable: this configuration can be relaxed automatically. Minimum attacker effort:

f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.insufficientDetail: The Loosen rule needs {need} distinct sources; the attacker controls {have}. Raising this threshold is one of the mitigations below.

note.autoEnforce: In Automatic mode, a suggestion that reaches a 100% learning score is accepted and enforced with no human in the loop — including suggestions that disable a violation or widen an entity.
note.unlearnable: The most severe violations (for example Null in request, unparsable content, bad HTTP version) are unlearnable: no suggestion is ever created and the rating is fixed at 5, so the control cannot be laundered away this way.
note.ratingCost: At rating {rating}, F5 slows learning and requires more different sources and sessions before it relaxes. The higher the rating, the more expensive the poisoning.
note.trustedOneSession: Trusted clients relax the policy with as little as one session; untrusted traffic needs many more distinct sources over a period. That asymmetry is the anti-poisoning design.
note.emptyTrusted: If the Trusted IP list is empty, the system treats all traffic as untrusted — so an empty list does not make you safe, it makes the untrusted thresholds the only defence.
note.attackerTrusted: These IPs are trusted, so the fast trusted-traffic rules apply — this is why you must never place untrusted-reachable ranges in the Trusted IP list.
note.lowerBound: This request count is a documented lower bound (one distinct hit per required source/session). A real campaign needs more, because the pattern must recur to raise the score.

mit.manual: Set Learning Mode to Manual (or Disabled) so no suggestion is applied without a human accepting it. This is the clean default for production.
mit.trustedOnly: Loosen only from trusted traffic where the option exists (Track Site Changes), and build policies from a known-good Trusted IP range rather than live untrusted traffic.
mit.raiseThresholds: Raise the untrusted Loosen thresholds (different sources, sessions, time spread) so untrusted traffic effectively never relaxes the policy during the learning period.
mit.keepOut: Never put internet-reachable or attacker-reachable ranges in the Trusted IP list — trusted traffic relaxes the policy with a single session.
mit.disableAfter: Once the policy is built and stable, disable automatic policy building (or Track Site Changes) so the policy stops learning from live traffic.

f5-awaf-learning-poisoning-estimator.untrustedActiveNote: This scenario loosens from untrusted traffic in Automatic mode — the exact configuration to avoid on an internet-facing policy.

as3-explainer-validator.name: AS3 declaration explainer
as3-explainer-validator.blurb: Paste the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/appsvcs/declare and it reads it back: whether it is a full AS3 request or an ADC-only declaration, the schemaVersion and metadata, and the Tenant to Application to resource tree with every class explained, while checking the structural rules F5 documents. Runs entirely in your browser.
as3-explainer-validator.inputLabel: An AS3 request or ADC declaration (JSON)
as3-explainer-validator.inputPlaceholder:
Paste an AS3 declaration, for example:
{ "class": "AS3", "action": "deploy", "declaration": { "class": "ADC", "schemaVersion": "3.0.0", "MyTenant": { "class": "Tenant", "MyApp": { "class": "Application", "service": { "class": "Service_HTTP", "virtualAddresses": ["10.0.0.1"] } } } } }
as3-explainer-validator.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded.
as3-explainer-validator.empty: Paste an AS3 request or an ADC declaration to see its structure explained and checked.
as3-explainer-validator.scopeNote: A structure explainer and sanity checker, not a full JSON-Schema validator: it checks the documented structural rules and explains the classes, but a declaration that passes here can still be rejected by AS3 itself. It never contacts a BIG-IP.

kind.as3Request: This is a full AS3 request (class AS3)
kind.adcOnly: This is an ADC-only declaration (class ADC, no AS3 wrapper)
kind.notAs3: This is not an AS3 or ADC declaration

finding.parseError: That looks like JSON but could not be parsed
finding.notAs3: The top-level object has no class of AS3 or ADC, so this is not an AS3 declaration. An AS3 request starts with class AS3; an ADC declaration starts with class ADC.
finding.adcOnly: This is an ADC-only declaration (no AS3 wrapper), so the action and persist options are not available. Wrap it in a class AS3 object to use them.
finding.missingSchema: The ADC declaration has no schemaVersion. AS3 requires it, and it is decoupled from the AS3 release version.
finding.noTenant: The declaration has no Tenant. A declaration must contain at least one object with class Tenant, which becomes a BIG-IP partition.
finding.emptyTenant: Tenant {tenant} has no Application. Each tenant must contain at least one object with class Application.
finding.emptyApp: Application {app} in tenant {tenant} has no resources. An application must contain at least one ADC resource, for example a Service or Pool.
finding.templateMismatch: Application {app} in tenant {tenant} uses template {template}, which requires a matching {needs} object named service (formerly serviceMain), but none was found.
finding.invalidName: The name "{name}" in {where} is not a valid AS3 name: names are 1 to 64 characters, alphanumeric or underscore, and must start with a letter.

as3-explainer-validator.unknownClass: (class not in this tool's catalog; still shown)

f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.name: AWAF false-positive triage
f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.blurb: The flip side of the poisoning estimator: relax a genuine Advanced WAF false positive correctly, with scope, and stop before relaxing a real attack. Pick a violation category, its average violation rating, and whether it is enforced, staged, or transparent, and get F5's rating-based verdict and the scoped fix. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented triage behaviour, not a probe. Read the violation, rating, and enforcement state from the request in Security > Event Logs > Application > Requests. It never contacts a BIG-IP.

cat.meta-char: Illegal meta character (in parameter, value, or URL)
cat.length: Illegal length (URL, query, POST data, request)
cat.xml-json-signature: Signature in XML or JSON payload

triage.likely-attack: Likely a real attack — do not relax

action.accept-if-confirmed: If you confirm it is legitimate, apply the scoped fix below (or accept the learning suggestion).
action.investigate-first: Examine the request in the event log first; a rating of 3 can go either way.
action.clear-do-not-relax: Clear the learning suggestion without changing the policy. Relaxing this would open a hole.

f5-awaf-false-positive-triage.remediationHeading: Scoped remediation (if it is a confirmed false positive)

rem.disableSignatureScoped: Disable the specific signature on the specific URL or parameter only, never for the whole policy.
rem.allowEntityBypass: Add the URL or parameter as an allowed entity so the check is bypassed just there.
rem.potentialFpDetection: Enable Potential False Positive Detection so the system learns request similarity and treats requests like the majority as benign, flagging outliers.
rem.stageSignature: Put the signature (back) into staging so it logs but does not block while you gather more traffic.
rem.allowMetaChar: Add the character to the allowed meta-characters of that parameter, value, or URL character set.
rem.addAllowedParameter: Add the parameter as an allowed parameter with the correct value type and length.
rem.addAllowedUrl: Add the URL to the allowed URLs.
rem.addAllowedFileType: Add the file type to the allowed file types.
rem.raiseLength: Raise the relevant length limit (URL, query string, POST data, or request) to fit legitimate traffic.
rem.markFileUploadParam: Add an explicit parameter with Value Type 'User-input value' and Data Type 'File Upload' so uploaded binary content is not scanned as text.
rem.attachContentProfile: Attach an XML or JSON content profile so the structured payload is parsed and signatures apply appropriately, rather than to raw bytes.
rem.allowCookieNotEnforced: Only if the application legitimately modifies the cookie, allow that specific cookie as not-enforced.
rem.investigateCookie: Cookie violations are usually real tampering; confirm the application actually changes the cookie before relaxing anything.
rem.adjustHttpComplianceSubcheck: Adjust the specific HTTP compliance sub-check only, if the deviation is legitimate for this application.
rem.urlOverride: Use a Microservices or URL-level override to relax the check for that one host and URL.

note.ratingBlocks: A rating of 4 or 5 blocks the request even when every detected violation has its Block flag off, because the combined rating marks it as a likely attack.
note.staged: This signature or entity is in staging: it logs but does not block. Treat the 'false positive' as a learning signal, validate over one to two weeks, then enforce.
note.transparent: The policy is in Transparent mode, so it blocks nothing. This is logged, not blocked; the fix matters for when you move to Blocking.
note.discipline: The rule that governs all of this: relax the policy only where a false positive actually occurred, never where a real attack caused the violation.
note.scopeIt: Always scope the fix to the specific URL or parameter. Disabling a signature or violation policy-wide trades one false positive for a blind spot everywhere else.
note.potentialFp: For attack-signature false positives, Potential False Positive Detection is the lightest-touch option: it lets the system decide by traffic similarity instead of you hand-disabling signatures.
note.unlearnable: Some rating-5 violations are unlearnable (for example Null in request): the policy will never relax them, so there is nothing to accept.

f5-awaf-request-log-triage.name: AWAF request-log triage
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.blurb: Paste an ASM request-log entry (syslog key-value or CEF) and it extracts the policy, the support ID for log correlation, the request status, the violation rating, the client IP, method, and URI, classifies each violation, and gives F5's rating-based verdict, then bridges to the false-positive triage tool. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.inputLabel: An ASM request-log entry (syslog key-value or CEF)
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.inputPlaceholder:
Paste a line like:
ASM:policy_name="prod",violations="Attack signature detected,Illegal meta character in parameter value",violation_rating="2",support_id="5268275531735896872",request_status="blocked",ip_client="192.0.2.9",method="GET",uri="/search"
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.runsLocally: Parsed in your browser. Nothing you paste is uploaded.
f5-awaf-request-log-triage.empty: Paste an ASM request-log line (key-value or CEF) to parse it and triage its violations.

verdict.likely-fp: Rating says: likely a false positive
verdict.likely-attack: Rating says: likely a real attack
verdict.no-rating: No violation rating in this log

note.noRating: This log has no violation_rating field (older versions omit it). Open the request in Security > Event Logs > Application > Requests to read its rating, then triage from there.
note.ratingAttack: The violation rating is {rating}. A 4 or 5 is most likely a real attack and blocks even with Block flags off, so clear any learning suggestion rather than relaxing the policy.
note.ratingFp: The violation rating is {rating}. A 1 or 2 is usually a genuine false positive, so if you confirm the request is legitimate you can apply the scoped fix in the false-positive triage tool.
note.ratingInvestigate: The violation rating is {rating}. A 3 is ambiguous; examine the request in the event log before you decide.
note.unparsed: This does not look like an ASM request-log line. Paste a syslog key-value line (starting with ASM:) or a CEF line (containing |F5|ASM|).
note.supportIdOpaque: The support ID is a correlation reference for finding this exact request in the event log. It does not encode the violations, so it cannot be decoded into them; this tool reads them from the log line instead.
note.bridge: Take each violation above into the false-positive triage tool with its category and the rating to get the scoped remediation.

f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.name: AWAF learning-suggestion interpreter
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.blurb: Ties the poisoning estimator and the false-positive triage together. Characterise a Traffic Learning suggestion and it tells you whether accepting it loosens or tightens the policy, whether a loosening is a false-positive fix or a security relaxation, and whether Automatic learning is about to enforce it for you. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.inputLabel: The learning suggestion you are weighing up
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-learning-suggestion-interpreter.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented learning behaviour, not a probe. Read the action, learning score, and average violation rating from the Traffic Learning screen. It never contacts a BIG-IP and does not depend on the exact REST suggestion JSON.

action.add-entity: Add a valid entity (URL / parameter / file type / cookie)
action.add-meta-char: Allow a meta-character on an entity
action.relax-attribute: Relax an entity attribute (e.g. longer length)
action.disable-signature: Disable or relax an attack signature
action.remove-wildcard: Remove a wildcard, add explicit entities

assessment.add-legitimate: Adding a legitimate entity the policy had not learned
assessment.fp-fix: Loosening that resolves a genuine false positive
assessment.likely-relaxing-attack: This loosening would relax a real attack

note.scoreRatingInverse: The learning score rises as the violation rating falls, so the lowest-rated suggestions (the most likely false positives) reach auto-accept fastest. That is by design, and it is also exactly what an attacker feeding low-rated violations drives up.
note.autoEnforce: In Automatic learning, a suggestion that reaches a 100% learning score is accepted and enforced automatically, with no human step.
note.poisoningVector: This is the poisoning vector: a relaxing loosening from untrusted traffic climbing toward auto-accept in Automatic mode. Model how far it is with the poisoning estimator, and consider Manual learning or trusted-only loosening.
note.discipline: Relax the policy only where a false positive occurred, never where a real attack caused the violation. The violation rating is how you tell the two apart.
note.enforceCheckFp: Enforcing moves the entity from staging to blocking, so confirm it has generated no false positives during staging before you accept.
note.tighteningSafe: Tightening increases the policy's specificity, so accepting it generally improves security rather than weakening it.
note.manualHuman: In Manual learning a human must accept every suggestion, so nothing is enforced without your review.
note.scoreNearAccept: At a {score}% learning score this suggestion is close to being accepted; check it deliberately rather than letting it through.

f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.name: AWAF signature accuracy/risk interpreter
f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.blurb: Read an attack signature's published Accuracy and Risk and it tells you how false-positive-prone it is, how damaging a real match would be, and the tuning move. F5 defines accuracy as false-positive susceptibility, so low accuracy means many false positives. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.runsLocally: Computed in your browser. Nothing is sent.
f5-awaf-signature-accuracy-risk.scopeNote: A deterministic model of F5's documented signature properties, not a per-ID database. Read Accuracy, Risk, and Systems from the signature's own entry under Security > Options > Application Security > Attack Signatures. It never contacts a BIG-IP.

system.in-stack: Yes — a system my app runs
system.not-in-stack: No — a system my app does not run

quadrant.reliable-high-stakes: Reliable and high-stakes — do not relax

rec.fp-prone-low-stakes: This is the prime relax candidate. Low accuracy means frequent false positives and low risk means little is lost if you relax it, so disabling it on the specific URL or parameter (or leaving it in staging) is reasonable once you have confirmed a false positive.
rec.fp-prone-dangerous: Investigate the actual request before you relax anything. Low accuracy makes false positives likely, but the potential damage means a genuine match must not be waved through. Do not blindly disable this one.
rec.reliable-high-stakes: Do not relax this. High accuracy means a match is very likely a genuine attack, and the potential damage is severe. Treat matches as real and keep it enforcing.
rec.reliable-low-stakes: Reliable but low-stakes. Matches are trustworthy, so relax only with a confirmed false positive, and even then scope it to the specific URL or parameter.

accuracyFp.low: F5 defines accuracy as a signature's susceptibility to false positives, and a Low accuracy signature has a high likelihood of false positives.
accuracyFp.medium: F5 defines accuracy as a signature's susceptibility to false positives, and a Medium accuracy signature has some likelihood of false positives.
accuracyFp.high: F5 defines accuracy as a signature's susceptibility to false positives, and a High accuracy signature has a low likelihood of false positives.

note.systemNotInStack: This signature targets a system your application does not run, so it is pure noise here. Rather than disable it one policy at a time, scope your signature set to your actual systems (for example only the databases, web servers, and languages you use).
note.systemUnknown: Confirm whether this signature's system applies to your stack; a signature for a system you do not run only generates false positives.
note.staged: This signature is in staging: it logs but does not block. Treat any false positive as a learning signal, and enforce it only once you are confident it is clean.
note.transparent: The policy is in Transparent mode, so it blocks nothing. This is logged, not blocked; the accuracy and risk matter for when you move to Blocking.
note.highAccuracyLever: If low-accuracy false positives are frequent, let accuracy guide which signatures you enforce. Accuracy is a documented signature property and a signature-set filter criterion, so a signature set weighted toward higher-accuracy signatures produces fewer false positives, because F5 defines accuracy as false-positive susceptibility.
note.disciplineHighRisk: Because this signature guards against a damaging attack, do not disable it just because it is false-positive-prone. Check the actual request first; the cost of relaxing a real match here is high.
note.scopeIt: Scope any change to the specific URL or parameter, or filter the signature set. Disabling a signature policy-wide trades one false positive for a blind spot everywhere else.

f5-awaf-policy-diff.name: AWAF policy-diff hole checker
f5-awaf-policy-diff.blurb: Paste a before and an after declarative WAF policy and it classifies every security-relevant change as a relaxation or a tightening, then answers the question that matters after tuning: did this open a hole? It flags relaxations that widen protection beyond a single entity apart from a properly-scoped single-entity allow. Runs entirely in your browser.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.inputLabel: Two declarative WAF policies to compare
f5-awaf-policy-diff.beforePlaceholder:
Paste the earlier policy JSON, e.g.
{ "policy": { "enforcementMode": "blocking", "data-guard": { "enabled": true } } }
f5-awaf-policy-diff.afterPlaceholder:
Paste the later policy JSON, e.g.
{ "policy": { "enforcementMode": "transparent", "data-guard": { "enabled": false } } }
f5-awaf-policy-diff.runsLocally: Compared in your browser. Neither policy is uploaded.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.scopeNote: A deterministic diff of the security-relevant sections F5's declarative policy schema defines. It reads enforcement mode, signature staging, X-Forwarded-For trust, Data Guard, CSRF, per-violation block flags, per-evasion enablement, and the URL / parameter / file-type entities. It never contacts a BIG-IP.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.empty: Paste a before and an after policy to see which changes opened a hole and which stayed scoped.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.parseError: One of the two inputs is not valid JSON. Check both policies and try again.
f5-awaf-policy-diff.notPolicy: One of the inputs does not look like a declarative WAF policy (expected a policy object, optionally wrapped in a "policy" key).

verdict.opened-hole: This opened a hole: a policy-wide relaxation is present
verdict.scoped-only: Scoped changes only: the widenings stay entity-scoped
verdict.no-change: No security-relevant change between the two policies

f5-awaf-policy-diff.policyWideHeading: Policy-wide relaxations (these widen beyond a single entity)
f5-awaf-policy-diff.scopedHeading: Scoped widenings (single-entity — the normal false-positive fix)

label.enforcementToTransparent: Enforcement switched to Transparent: the whole policy stops blocking, regardless of per-violation block flags.
label.enforcementToBlocking: Enforcement switched to Blocking: the policy now enforces its block flags.
label.sigStagingOn: Signature staging turned on: signatures log but no longer block until enforced.
label.sigStagingOff: Signature staging turned off: signatures now enforce.
label.trustXffOn: X-Forwarded-For trust turned on: the policy now trusts a client-supplied header for the source IP, which is spoofable.
label.trustXffOff: X-Forwarded-For trust turned off: the policy no longer trusts the client-supplied header.
label.dataGuardOff: Data Guard turned off: responses are no longer masked for sensitive data leakage.
label.dataGuardOn: Data Guard turned on: responses are masked for sensitive data.
label.wildcardAdded: Wildcard {type} added: {name} — this matches many, not one entity.

do-explainer-validator.name: DO declaration explainer + validator
do-explainer-validator.blurb: Paste the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/declarative-onboarding and it reads it back: whether it is a DO request wrapper or a bare Device declaration, the top-level options, and the Common tenant's class-objects grouped by the phase DO onboards them in, with every class named and explained. It also checks the structural rules F5 documents and flags the gotchas that bite in production, from the DO 1.36 allowService default change to a root user missing its oldPassword. DO is the sibling of AS3: DO does the L1-L3 onboarding, AS3 the L4-L7 services.
do-explainer-validator.inputLabel: A DO declaration (Device JSON) or a class DO request wrapper
do-explainer-validator.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
do-explainer-validator.empty: Paste a DO declaration to see it explained.
do-explainer-validator.scopeNote: This is a structure explainer and sanity checker, not a full JSON-Schema validator. It never contacts a BIG-IP or BIG-IQ and nothing you paste leaves the browser. A declaration that passes here can still be rejected by DO itself.
do-explainer-validator.clusterNote: This declaration includes clustering classes, so it onboards one member of a high-availability pair or group.
do-explainer-validator.walkOrderNote: Grouped by the phase DO effectively onboards them in: licensing and provisioning gate the modules, system identity is foundational, networking builds the data plane, then clustering joins the box to its peers.
do-explainer-validator.unknownClass: (class not in this tool's catalog, but still reported)

req.noDeclaration: no declaration found in the wrapper

finding.notDo: This is not a DO declaration. Paste a Device declaration, or a class DO request wrapper.
finding.deviceOnly: This is a bare Device declaration, as POSTed straight to a BIG-IP (a class DO wrapper with a targetHost is used against a BIG-IQ).
finding.missingSchema: No schemaVersion. A DO declaration should declare the schemaVersion it targets.
finding.noCommon: No Common tenant found. A DO declaration must contain a single tenant named Common.
finding.tenantNotCommon: The tenant is named "{name}", but DO requires the tenant to be named Common.
finding.emptyCommon: The Common tenant has no onboarding objects.
finding.hostnameConflict: hostname is set on both Common and a System class. They are mutually exclusive; set it in one place only.
finding.rootOldPassword: The root user "{name}" has no oldPassword. Changing the root password requires its existing oldPassword.
finding.allowServiceDefault: The self IP "{name}" sets no allowService. Since DO 1.36 the default changed from `default` to `none`, so it now allows no services unless you set them explicitly.
finding.asyncNote: async is true, so DO returns a 202 with a task id immediately; poll the task with GET until it completes.

telemetry-streaming-explainer.name: Telemetry Streaming (TS) explainer
telemetry-streaming-explainer.blurb: Paste the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/telemetry/declare and it reads it back: it confirms the top-level Telemetry class, reads the optional Controls, and walks every class-object grouped by its role in the telemetry pipeline: the data sources that produce telemetry (system pollers, event listeners), the consumers that forward it out (Splunk, Azure, AWS, DataDog, Prometheus, and the rest of the catalogue), and the grouping and endpoint classes. It flags the pipeline gaps that make a declaration succeed but do nothing. TS is the third F5 Automation Toolchain extension: AS3 and DO configure the box, TS observes it.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.inputLabel: A TS declaration (the JSON you POST to /mgmt/shared/telemetry/declare)
telemetry-streaming-explainer.runsLocally: Decoding runs locally in your browser. Nothing you paste leaves this page.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.empty: Paste a TS declaration to see it explained.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.scopeNote: This is a structure explainer and sanity checker, not a full JSON-Schema validator. It never contacts a BIG-IP and nothing you paste leaves the browser. A declaration that passes here can still be rejected by TS itself. Note: F5 has placed Telemetry Streaming in maintenance mode; it remains supported with critical and security fixes, with no deprecation planned.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.walkOrderNote: Grouped by each class's role: data sources produce telemetry (system pollers collect statistics, event listeners ingest logs), consumers forward it to an external system, and the supporting classes group and extend the rest. A working declaration needs at least one source and at least one consumer.
telemetry-streaming-explainer.unknownClass: (class not in this tool's catalog, but still reported)

finding.notTelemetry: This is not a TS declaration. The top-level class must be Telemetry.
finding.noSource: No data source. Add a Telemetry_System (with a systemPoller) or a Telemetry_Listener, or the declaration collects nothing to forward.
finding.noConsumer: No consumer. Add a Telemetry_Consumer (push) or Telemetry_Pull_Consumer (pull), or the telemetry has nowhere to go.
finding.systemWithoutPoller: The Telemetry_System "{name}" has no systemPoller (and no iHealthPoller). To collect metrics, a Telemetry_System must define systemPoller.
finding.consumerMissingType: The consumer "{name}" has no type. A Telemetry_Consumer must set a type to select its target.
finding.consumerUnknownType: The consumer "{name}" has type "{type}", which is not in this tool's catalogue of TS consumer types (it may be newer than this build, or a typo).
finding.namespacesPresent: This declaration uses Telemetry_Namespace: pollers, listeners, and consumers inside a namespace operate as an isolated unit and only see each other.
finding.controlsNote: Controls is set with logLevel "{logLevel}" (used for logging and debugging; set debug true to send a test event to your consumers).

privacy_page.title: Your data, and what this site does with it
privacy_page.lede: A plain-language account of exactly what this website does and does not do with your information. It is written to be accurate rather than merely reassuring, because under regulations like the GDPR and Brazil's LGPD it is the accurate disclosure that matters.
privacy_page.short1: This site sets no cookies, runs no analytics, uses no advertising, and contains no third-party trackers or fingerprinting. Nothing follows you across the web.
privacy_page.short2: It does not sell, share, or build profiles of its visitors, and it asks you for nothing in order to read it.
privacy_page.short3: The interactive tools run entirely in your browser. What you paste or type into them stays on your device and is not transmitted anywhere.
privacy_page.short4: Only three things involve data at all, and each is explained below: a single theme setting saved in your own browser, the standard connection logs that every website visit creates at the hosting layer, and any message you choose to send by email.
privacy_page.controllerBody: This is the personal website of Rodolfo Nützmann (ronutz.com). For any privacy question, or to exercise any of the rights described below, write to {email}. Enquiries are handled directly by Rodolfo Nützmann, as the data controller.
privacy_page.noTrackTitle: No cookies, no tracking, no analytics
privacy_page.noTrackBody: This site sets no cookies of any kind. It uses no analytics service (such as Google Analytics or Plausible), no advertising network, no social-media tracking pixels, and no device fingerprinting. Your visit is not measured, profiled, or shared with anyone for those purposes.
privacy_page.browserTitle: What is stored in your browser
privacy_page.browserBody: One small thing: your chosen visual theme, saved in your browser's local storage so the site can remember it the next time you visit. It is a convenience setting that stays on your own device, is never sent to this site or to anyone else, and can be cleared at any time through your browser. No other data about you is stored on your device.
privacy_page.hostingBody: The site is delivered through Cloudflare, which provides its hosting and content delivery. As with any website, loading a page means your device connects to a server, and Cloudflare, acting as the hosting provider, processes basic connection data, including your IP address and standard request details, in order to deliver the pages to you and to protect the site against abuse and attack. This processing is necessary to operate and secure the site (a legitimate interest under GDPR Article 6(1)(f), and the corresponding bases under LGPD Articles 7 and 10). It is retained only briefly by the provider and is not used here to identify or track you.
privacy_page.toolsTitle: The tools run in your browser
privacy_page.toolsBody: The decoders and calculators on this site, for JWTs, IP addresses, certificates and the rest, compute entirely within your browser. The values you enter are processed on your own device and are never sent to a server. A separate programmatic interface (an API) is offered for automation and integrations; by design it is stateless and logs none of the queries or request bodies sent to it.
privacy_page.contactBody: The contact form does not submit to any server. Instead it opens a pre-filled message in your own email application, which you then send yourself from your own account, so nothing passes through a third party. If you do write, whether through the form or directly, I receive the information you include, such as your name, email address and message, and use it only to reply to you. I keep it only for as long as needed to deal with your enquiry, do not use it for marketing, and do not share it.
privacy_page.intlBody: Because Cloudflare operates a global network, the connection-level processing described above may take place on servers located outside your own country, including outside Brazil and the European Economic Area. Where personal data is handled internationally in this way, it is processed under the safeguards offered by the provider concerned.
privacy_page.childrenBody: This site is intended for a professional, general audience. It is not directed at children and does not knowingly collect personal data from them.
privacy_page.rightsBody: Depending on where you live, you have rights over your personal data, including to access it, to have it corrected or deleted, to object to or restrict its processing, to request a copy in a portable form, and to be told how it is handled. To exercise any of these, simply write to {email}. Because so little personal data is ever processed here, in practice there is usually very little to act on, but the channel is always open.
privacy_page.rightsGdpr: If you are in the European Economic Area or the United Kingdom, you also have the right to lodge a complaint with your local data protection supervisory authority.
privacy_page.rightsLgpd: If you are in Brazil, you hold the rights set out in Article 18 of the LGPD, and may also contact the national data protection authority (the ANPD).
privacy_page.changesBody: If this notice changes, the revised version will be published on this page and the date below will be updated. Material changes will not be applied retroactively.
privacy_page.prefsTitle: Theme, language, and what this site remembers
privacy_page.prefsBody: Your theme choice is stored only in this browser's localStorage, on your device, and applied before first paint on your next visit; there is no cookie, no account, and no server that ever sees it, which also means it cannot follow you: a private window, cleared site data, or a different browser or device starts you back at the default theme by design. Your language is not stored anywhere at all, because it lives in the address itself: /en/ and /pt-BR/ are different pages, so bookmarking or sharing a URL in your language keeps it, while visiting the bare domain serves the default. That is the whole trade: with no accounts and no tracking, preferences can only live where you can see and delete them.
privacy_page.shortcutsBody: The site has a handful of single-key shortcuts for quick navigation, active on every page. Press b for the boss key, which hides whatever you are reading behind a convincing 1980s work application until you press any key or click; t jumps to the Tools index; l to the Learn index; m opens the Mega Brain console; and z opens Buzzword Bingo. These are deliberately inert whenever you are typing: if the focus is in a text field, a paste box, a search input, or any editable area, the keystroke belongs to that field and the shortcut does nothing, so nothing you type is ever swallowed. They are also ignored when a modifier key is held, so your browser's own combinations such as Ctrl+T for a new tab are never shadowed. The shortcuts run entirely in your browser, add no tracking, and are purely a convenience you can ignore.

about.lede: Network and security training delivered by a practitioner who has spent his career on the implementation side, not just the slides. Based in São Paulo, teaching globally, in Portuguese and English.

now.title: What I do now
now.body: I deliver official, certified instructor-led training across four security and networking platforms. There are hands-on labs on real systems, and for people who already know the fundamentals and came to practice, that is exactly the point: real environments to work in. For groups newer to a technology, the time goes first into building the foundations the topic rests on, so the hands-on work makes sense and people leave understanding why things behave the way they do, not just which buttons to press. Both are equally valuable, and the balance shifts with who is in the room. Teaching and technical enablement have been part of my work since the late 1990s, on a career that began on the implementation side in 1996.
now.teachesValue: Virtual and on-site instructor-led training, globally

platforms.title: Four platforms, taught in depth
platforms.body: Being certified across four vendors means I can teach each one properly and compare them honestly. These are the platforms I am currently authorized to deliver official training for.
platforms.f5: BIG-IP across the full instructor-led curriculum: LTM, DNS, Advanced WAF (ASM), APM, AFM, SSL Orchestrator, automation, and troubleshooting.
platforms.fortinet: Fortinet certified training, including the FCP track.
platforms.extreme: Extreme Networks switching, SD-WAN, and API and automation tracks, across installation, configuration, management, and troubleshooting.

path.title: The path here
path.intro: My career began on the vendor and implementation side in 1996, and stayed there for two decades before I focused fully on training. That implementation background is the reason the training lands: I have built, broken, and fixed these systems in production.
path.cabletron: Network engineer and certified instructor. Non-Ethernet and Ethernet networks, campus networks, IP routing.
path.riverstone: Product support engineer and knowledge-base coordinator in California. Gigabit Ethernet, metropolitan area networks, IP routing, BGP. Third-level escalation, customer scenario recreation, and regression testing.
path.cisco: Network consulting engineer, acting as the single point of contact for key customers including SERPRO and Correios. Escalation and customer-satisfaction management.
path.enterasys: Network engineer and instructor. Enterprise networks, IP routing, user access control and NAC, IDS and IPS.
path.juniper: Channel support engineer and instructor for enterprise switches and firewalls. Technical sales enablement and Junos-SRX operations training.
path.f5channel: Channel-facing sales and pre-sales engineering for F5, providing technical enablement, proof-of-concept execution, and partner training across distribution.
path.rededucation: Authorized training instructor delivering official certified courses across F5, Fortinet, Extreme Networks, and Netskope to organizations worldwide.

origins.title: Where it started
origins.body: Before the formal career, there was the curiosity. Through the early 1990s I was importing and assembling computers, running and using bulletin board systems, and working across DOS, Unix, Linux, Netware, early Ethernet, X.25, and dial-up internet. By 1995 that hands-on work had become a first formal role building an electronic-data-interchange system. The thread from then to now is the same: take something genuinely complex and make it work, then make it make sense to someone else.

approach.title: How I teach
approach.body: I chose to specialize in training because it combines deep technical knowledge with the part of the work I enjoy most: explaining complex concepts simply. The strongest sessions connect each concept to the job the learner actually has to do, so the content has somewhere to land. Real-world examples, real labs, and a focus on understanding over memorization.

cta.title: Start with the concepts
cta.body: The Learn section explains the ideas behind the tools, and the tools let you work with them directly, all in your browser.

recognition.title: Recognition
recognition.body: Recognized as an F5 DevCentral MVP for three consecutive years, in 2022, 2023, and 2024, for contributions to the F5 technical community.

history.indexLede: Three eras, one through-line: take something genuinely complex, make it work, then make it make sense to someone else.

pre1996.title: The curiosity
pre1996.subtitle: Importing parts, assembling machines, and dialing into the early internet, before any of it was a job.
pre1996.intro: Every technical career has a before. Before the vendor badges and the certifications, there was a teenager taking machines apart to understand how they worked, and a young autonomous professional turning that understanding into a living. This is where the instinct that drives everything else was formed.
pre1996.s1Body: From 1991, the work was autonomous and hands-on: importing computer parts from the United States, assembling and selling custom-built personal computers, and installing, configuring, and troubleshooting them for whoever needed help. This was the era of building a machine from components and making it run, not buying one off a shelf. The systems of the day passed through these hands directly: DOS and CP/M, BASIC, Turbo Pascal, early Windows and office applications, and the database tools that ran small businesses then, Clipper and dBase.
pre1996.s2Body: Networking did not begin with the web. Long before broadband, the connective tissue was different and harder to work with: Novell NetWare for local servers, bulletin board systems for community and file exchange, early Ethernet networks, and the wide-area technologies of the time, X.25 (known in Brazil as RENPAC) and Frame Relay. When the internet did arrive, it arrived over a modem, reached through a shell account, SLIP, or PPP. Working across all of it meant understanding networks from the wire up, an understanding that later made enterprise networking feel like familiar ground.
pre1996.s3Title: BBS, phreaking, and the academic internet
pre1996.s3Body: The genuine curiosity of the era went beyond paid work. The explorations of those years included running and using bulletin board systems, the phone-network tinkering of the phreaking scene, hands-on UNIX, and early access to the academic internet. This was learning by doing, in a community that shared knowledge because there was nowhere else to get it. That habit, of digging until something is genuinely understood and then passing it on, never went away.
pre1996.s4Body: In 1995, the autonomous work converged into a first formal position. At INTELECTA, a company incubated by SEBRAE-SP, the project was an electronic-data-interchange system for the commercialization of medical and hospital supplies, built on Novell NetWare servers and the PCBoard bulletin board system. It was a real system, solving a real commercial problem, and it marked the point where the hobby and the trade became a profession.
pre1996.closer: By the end of 1995, the foundation was set: a person who understood machines and networks from first principles, who learned by building, and who already had the instinct to explain. Everything that followed, the vendors, the certifications, the global classrooms, was built on this.

era19962020.title: The practitioner
era19962020.subtitle: Two decades inside the networking and security industry, building, breaking, and fixing the systems that later became the curriculum.
era19962020.intro: This is the long middle of the story, and the reason the teaching that came later carries weight. For roughly two decades, the work was implementation: designing networks, standing them up, troubleshooting them under pressure, and being the person called when something critical broke. The journey ran through some of the defining names in networking, on two continents.
era19962020.s1Body: The formal career began in 1996 at Cabletron Systems, then a leading networking equipment vendor based in Rochester, New Hampshire. Over four and a half years, the role spanned field engineering and post-sales support, systems engineering and pre-sales, and, from 1997, certified instruction. The subject matter was the enterprise LAN and WAN of the era: switches, routers, Wi-Fi and WLAN, network management, NAC and UAC, and stateful-inspection firewalls. In 2000, Cabletron reorganized into four companies, and the part this career touched became Enterasys Networks.
era19962020.s2Body: The next chapter was in California. Riverstone Networks, a Cabletron spin-off building metropolitan-area network equipment, brought a move to Santa Clara on an H1-B1 work visa. The role was Tier-III product support engineer and knowledge-management coordinator: third-level technical escalation, recreating customer scenarios in the lab, regression testing and bug verification, and building the knowledge base that the rest of support relied on. The technologies were the backbone of the early-2000s carrier and metro world: Gigabit Ethernet, MPLS, metropolitan-area switching and routing, and BGP. This period also produced a formal milestone: as part of the visa process, a United States evaluation in 2001 recognized an educational background equivalent to a bachelor's degree in computer science and a bachelor's degree in business administration.
era19962020.s3Body: Back in Brazil, and contracted through Cisco Professional Services, the role shifted toward the customer relationship at its most demanding. As a high-touch operations manager and single point of contact, the work was post-sales customer-satisfaction management for two of Brazil's largest federal entities, SERPRO, the government's data-processing agency, and ECT-Correios, the national postal service. The technical surface was Catalyst switches and routers, PIX firewalls, and CSS, CSM, and ACE load balancers, but the real work was escalation management and keeping critical national infrastructure running smoothly.
era19962020.s4Body: The second half of the decade returned to the vendor side as an enterprise LAN subject-matter expert. At Enterasys from 2005, the focus was switches and routers, Wi-Fi and WLAN, network management, NAC and UAC, and intrusion detection and prevention, across solution design, implementation, auditing, and training. From 2009, at Juniper Networks through Professional Services for LATAM, the role was new-product advocacy for the strategic partner Telefónica Empresas in Brazil, centered on EX switches and SRX firewalls, with operations training on Junos-SRX delivered at Level 3 and Impsat, now Lumen.
era19962020.s5Title: The channel and consulting years, 2010 to 2020
era19962020.s5Body: The final decade of this era moved fluidly between roles, all of them building toward the instructor the story arrives at. There were stints as a network and security engineer through resellers and distributors, deepening expertise in enterprise switching and routing, firewalls and next-generation firewalls, SSL-VPN, user access control, WAN acceleration, and internet load-balancing across Juniper and Cisco solutions. Crucially, this period included the pivot toward F5, beginning work with F5 BIG-IP that would define the years to come. Across all of it, technical training was a constant thread, never absent from any role.
era19962020.closer: By 2020, the picture was complete: someone who had not just studied these systems but lived inside them for twenty years, on the vendor side and the customer side, in design and in crisis. That is the difference an implementer brings to a classroom. When this person explains why a configuration behaves a certain way, it is because they have seen it behave that way, at three in the morning, with a critical service waiting.

era2020present.title: The instructor
era2020present.subtitle: Full-time technical training, delivered globally, expanding platform by platform.
era2020present.intro: In 2020, a career-long thread became the whole cloth. After two decades where teaching accompanied every role, training became the work itself: full-time, official, certified instruction delivered to professionals and teams across the world. What makes this era distinct is not just the focus, but the deliberate, dated expansion across platforms, each one added on top of deep practitioner experience rather than in place of it.
era2020present.s1Body: Since 2020, the work has been delivering official instructor-led training, remotely and in person, in Portuguese and English, to audiences around the globe. The delivery reaches Australia, Singapore, India, Central Europe, the United States, and Brazil. Brazilian and German citizenship, together with travel clearance to the United States and the United Kingdom, make that global reach practical: unrestricted eligibility to work across the European Union and Mercosur, and the freedom to go where the classroom is.
era2020present.s2Body: F5 has been the anchor since the beginning of this era, building on work with BIG-IP that reaches back over a decade. The authorized curriculum covers the full BIG-IP instructor-led catalog: Local Traffic Manager, DNS, Advanced WAF, Access Policy Manager, Advanced Firewall Manager, SSL Orchestrator, BIG-IQ, iRules development, automation, and troubleshooting, ranging from one-day to four-day courses. Alongside the teaching runs a practical complement: managed F5 BIG-IP lab environments, so that hands-on practice always has somewhere real to happen.
era2020present.s3Body: What distinguishes recent years is a clear, dated progression. Extreme Networks training was added from 2021, covering EXOS switching, SD-WAN, and automation. Fortinet followed from 2024, centered on the FCP FortiGate Administrator track. Netskope joined from 2025, covering Netskope Security Cloud operation, administration, implementation, and integration. Four platforms, each added deliberately, each resting on the same two decades of network and security implementation underneath.
era2020present.s4Title: Through Red Education, to the world
era2020present.s4Body: The primary vehicle for this global delivery is Red Education, an authorized training center through which official certified courses reach organizations across regions. The role is global technical training instructor, delivering to the kind of international audiences that a São Paulo base, two passports, and a genuine love of travel make possible. The standing relationship turns individual expertise into something organizations worldwide can book and rely on.
era2020present.closer: This is where the through-line of the whole history pays off. The curiosity of the early years became the implementation depth of the practitioner years, and that depth is now what every student receives: not a presenter reading slides, but someone who built these systems explaining how they actually work, and why.

vendors.indexLede: The networking and security companies whose technology shaped this career, from the wire up. These are past relationships; the platforms taught today are listed under Training.

cabletron.name: Cabletron and Enterasys
cabletron.tagline: Where the career began, and the enterprise LAN was learned from the ground up.
cabletron.intro: The longest single thread in this history runs through one company and its successor. Cabletron Systems, a leading networking equipment vendor based in Rochester, New Hampshire, was the first employer, starting in 1996. When Cabletron reorganized into four companies in 2000, the line carried forward as Enterasys Networks, and the relationship resumed there from 2005. Together these span the formative decade of enterprise networking expertise.
cabletron.s1Body: Over four and a half years, the role moved through the full arc of vendor engineering: field engineering and post-sales support, systems engineering and pre-sales, and, from 1997, certified instruction. The daily work was hands-on and physical: installing, configuring, troubleshooting, upgrading, migrating, and sometimes physically relocating the secure, redundant, resilient, managed networks that ran São Paulo's enterprises, LAN, campus LAN, and WAN alike. The WAN side was built on Frame Relay and fractional-E1 leased lines, the LP circuits of the Brazilian carriers, reaching up to ATM and Packet over SONET on the faster links, and leaned mostly on Cisco routers, from the period when Cabletron partnered with Cisco on routing before it built its own. This was where the discipline of solution design, implementation, auditing, troubleshooting, and escalation management was first practised, and where teaching first entered the work.
cabletron.s2Body: Returning to the lineage as a services and support manager, the focus was the Secure Routing product line, with Enterasys as the regional focal point for Brazil. The work combined high-level customer support, complex troubleshooting, and escalation management, including the demanding escalations that came in from a key account, Petrobras in São Paulo, with localized product management alongside. The technical surface had grown to include network management, NAC and UAC, intrusion detection and prevention, and SIEM, across enterprise switching and routing. Enterasys was later acquired by Extreme Networks in 2013, closing a circle that connects this early work to a platform still taught today.
cabletron.certs: Cabletron Systems Engineer (CSE), 1999. Enterasys Systems Engineer (ESE), 2000 and 2007. Enterasys Certified Internetworking Engineer (ECIE), 2007.
cabletron.lineageDesc: Cabletron Systems, based in Rochester New Hampshire, split into four companies in 2000: Enterasys, Riverstone, Aprisma, and GNTS. Enterasys was acquired by Extreme Networks in 2013. Riverstone was acquired by Alcatel-Lucent in 2006.
cabletron.s3Body: One thread from the Enterasys years deserves recording precisely, because it is easy to overstate. This was the height of policy-based networking, Enterasys built its Secure Networks architecture around role-based access and 802.1X, and the work sat in close, constant contact with the demanding world of contact-center networks and the Avaya and Cisco IP-telephony systems running on top of them. The familiarity ran deep, but it was familiarity from the network side. The role was never unified-communications engineering; it carried no telephony certification and no hands-on UC work. What it delivered instead was the layer all of that depends on: a secure, redundant, resilient, monitored, guaranteed always-on superhighway for voice and mission-critical traffic. Being clear about where that line sits is part of describing the work honestly.
cabletron.ssrBody: One product from those years changed the trajectory. Cabletron's SmartSwitch Router, the SSR, came out of its 1998 acquisition of Yago Systems, an early Layer-3 switch that put wire-speed routing where the enterprise needed it. Becoming one of the world's top SSR specialists, and teaching it across Brazil to partners and customers, was the expertise that would carry the career out of Brazil entirely: the SSR line became Riverstone Networks, based in Santa Clara, and that is where it led next.
cabletron.fieldBody: None of this happened at a desk. It meant pulling cable under the petrol-ship pier at São Sebastião for Petrobras, alongside the country manager and nearly the entire local team, because the deadline and the late-fee clause left no other option. It meant climbing metal structures to mount antennas at the top, and crawling through tight, dusty spaces, always in full business attire, designer tie included. It meant constant travel by car, plane, and taxi, and waking up more than once not sure which city it was. It also meant meeting a great many people and making friends and acquaintances along the way. The work was physical, relentless, and, in its own way, a privilege.
cabletron.customersBody: Where the work went is its own kind of résumé. The customer list ran across the Brazilian economy of the era: the datacentres of national and international banks and credit-card processors, the BM&F futures exchange, and investment and stock-trading brokers; Rede Globo across many sites in Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo, Editora Abril, and print and cable-TV media; Petrobras throughout both states, Votorantim and VCP, and chemical and pharmaceutical industry; airlines, national bus-line operators, insurers, call centres, retail and fast-food chains, construction and development firms; mobile-phone and cable operators; federal, state, and municipal government; and large, geographically distributed university campuses. Few weeks looked alike.
cabletron.multiprotoBody: The enterprise network of the late 1990s was not the Ethernet-and-IP monoculture it would later become. The wire itself was as likely to be Token Ring, or an FDDI or CDDI fibre-and-copper ring backbone, as it was to be Ethernet. And IP was only one protocol among many, sharing the same segments with IBM's SNA, DEC's DECnet, Xerox's XNS, AppleTalk, and Novell's IPX. IPX, a descendant of XNS, was a notorious source of broadcast storms: it ran in any of four different Ethernet frame types, 802.3 “raw”, 802.2, SNAP, and Ethernet II, and a segment where those were mismatched, or where its SAP service broadcasts went unchecked, could drown itself in overhead while stations on the wrong frame type sat unable to talk at all. Making these protocols coexist on one infrastructure, and keeping it stable, was the daily reality.

juniper.name: NetScreen and Juniper
juniper.tagline: Enterprise switching and the security gateways that grew from NetScreen into the Juniper SRX line.
juniper.intro: Juniper Networks acquired NetScreen, the firewall company, in 2004, and NetScreen's security technology became the foundation of Juniper's secure-gateway line. Working with both reflects one continuous lineage: the NetScreen SSG firewalls and the SRX gateways that succeeded them. The relationship ran directly through Juniper from 2009, and continued through the reseller channel afterward.
juniper.s1Body: Contracted through Juniper Professional Services for Brazil, the role was new-product advocacy for the newly introduced enterprise lines: the SRX-series secure gateways and EX-series switches. The primary engagement was with the strategic partner Telefónica Empresas in Brazil. Beyond pre-sales advocacy, this period delivered real instruction: JUNOS networking-operating-system and SRX secure-gateway operations training for channel partners and customers, including operations training for the Level 3 Communications network operations center staff in Brazil and Argentina.
juniper.s2Body: The Juniper and NetScreen relationship continued through systems integrators and resellers. At CYLK, the work spanned configuration development, proof-of-concept and interoperability testing, and live implementation and troubleshooting across Juniper, NetScreen, and F5. At TDec, Juniper joined a multi-vendor consulting and training practice. The hands-on product range across these years was broad: EX switches, SRX and NetScreen SSG firewalls, SA SSL-VPN, J-series routers, and AX managed Wi-Fi.
juniper.certs: Juniper Networks Sales Specialist, Enterprise Networking (JNSS-EN), 2010. Juniper Networks Sales Associate, Enterprise Networking (JNSA-EN), 2010.
juniper.lineageDesc: NetScreen, maker of the SSG firewalls, was acquired by Juniper Networks in 2004 and became the foundation of Juniper's SRX secure-gateway line.

riverstone.name: Riverstone Networks
riverstone.tagline: Two years in Santa Clara, at the carrier and metropolitan-network edge.
riverstone.intro: The one chapter of this career set entirely outside Brazil. Riverstone Networks, a metropolitan-area-network equipment vendor based in Santa Clara, California, brought a move to the United States on an H1-B1 work visa. Riverstone had an unusual lineage of its own: originally a company called Yago, acquired by Cabletron around 1998, and re-emerged as an independent company through a spin-off in 2001. It was later acquired by Alcatel-Lucent in 2006.
riverstone.s1Body: The role carried two titles across two years: product support engineer and knowledge-management coordinator. As a Tier-III technical-support specialist, the work was third-level escalation, recreating customer scenarios in the lab, regression testing, and bug verification. As knowledge-management coordinator, it was building and organizing the knowledge base the rest of support relied on, alongside product-marketing support and internal training. This was the deepest technical-support work of the career, at the level where the hardest problems escalate.
riverstone.s2Body: The technologies were the backbone of the early-2000s carrier and metro era: Gigabit Ethernet, MPLS, metropolitan-area switching and routing, and wide-area IP routing including BGP. Working at the vendor's third-level support meant seeing how these systems behaved at the edges, under the conditions that only surface in production at scale. It also delivered formal instruction: Riverstone concepts, features, and configuration training, and customer-support issue-identification and escalation-procedure training.
riverstone.certs: Riverstone Certified Networking Professional (RCNP), 2001. United States educational-equivalency evaluation recognizing a background equivalent to a bachelor's degree in computer science and a bachelor's degree in business administration, completed in 2001 as part of the H1-B1 visa process.
riverstone.lineageDesc: Riverstone Networks began as a company called Yago, was acquired by Cabletron Systems around 1998, re-emerged as an independent company through a spin-off in 2001, and was acquired by Alcatel-Lucent in 2006.

cisco.name: Cisco Systems
cisco.tagline: Keeping critical national infrastructure running, as the single point of contact for Brazil's federal customers.
cisco.intro: Contracted through Cisco Professional Services in Brazil, this was the career at its most operationally demanding, where the work was less about any single technology and more about keeping critical systems running for customers who could not afford downtime.
cisco.s1Body: As a high-touch operations manager and single point of contact, the role was post-sales customer-satisfaction management for two of Brazil's largest federal entities: SERPRO, the government's data-processing agency, and ECT-Correios, the national postal service, across Brasília and São Paulo. The technical surface included Catalyst switches and routers, PIX firewalls, and CSS, CSM, and ACE content and load-balancing appliances. The real discipline, though, was escalation management and crisis handling, maintaining high satisfaction for accounts where the stakes were national.
cisco.s2Body: The Cisco relationship also produced formal training delivery: IOS and CatOS concepts and operations, and Layer 2 and Layer 3 networking concepts, operations, and troubleshooting, in multi-day courses. This complemented the deep operational experience with the structured instruction that would later become the whole focus of the career.
cisco.certs: Cisco Certified Network Associate (CCNA), 2000 and 2005. Cisco Internetwork Troubleshooting (CIT), 2003.

paloalto.name: Palo Alto Networks
paloalto.tagline: Next-generation firewalls, through the reseller channel and the classroom.
paloalto.intro: A focused relationship centered on next-generation firewall technology, carried through the reseller and integrator channel. This is a past engagement; Palo Alto is not among the platforms taught today, but the work was real and certified.
paloalto.s1Body: At TDec Network Group, Palo Alto joined a multi-vendor consulting and training practice spanning systems engineering, solutions architecture, application enablement, implementation, audits, and troubleshooting. The relationship continued through Cipher Security in 2015 for pre-sales and post-sales work. The technical focus was next-generation firewalls and the Panorama management platform, the core of Palo Alto's enterprise security offering at the time.
paloalto.s2Body: This period included formal training delivery: a PAN-OS 6.0 bootcamp, an early instance of the same practitioner-led teaching that continues today. Combined with the certifications earned in 2014, this established genuine depth in the platform, even as the career's focus later concentrated on other technologies.
paloalto.certs: Palo Alto Certified Network Security Engineer (CNSE 5.1), 2014. Palo Alto Networks Accredited Configuration Engineer (ACE), 2014. Palo Alto Networks Accredited Sales Expert (ASE), 2014. Note: these certifications are historical and are not maintained as current.

ironport.name: IronPort Systems
ironport.tagline: A brief chapter with an email-security startup, years before it became part of Cisco.
ironport.intro: Worth recording on its own rather than as a footnote. IronPort Systems was an independent email-security-appliance startup based in San Bruno, California, years before Cisco acquired it in 2007. This engagement predates that acquisition entirely, so it was a relationship with an independent company and its own technology, not with Cisco.
ironport.s1Body: Channel development and pre-sales technical consulting for IronPort's C-Series email-security appliances, across 2004 and 2005. It was the early-stage mix a young security vendor needs in a new market: building channel interest, and giving the technical pre-sales support that turns interest into deployments.
ironport.s2Body: The C-Series were purpose-built email-security appliances, known for reputation-based filtering at a time when spam and email-borne threats were escalating fast. Cisco acquired the company in 2007 and folded the technology into what became its Email Security Appliance line. That later history is why the name is familiar today, but the engagement here was with the independent company that came before it.
ironport.certs: No certification track. This was a short channel and pre-sales engagement.

extreme.name: Extreme Networks
extreme.tagline: Building RNP's metropolitan research networks, and the one vendor here that is also a platform taught today.
extreme.intro: This one closes a circle. Enterasys, the second half of the very first chapter of this career, was acquired by Extreme Networks in 2013, and the same year brought a return to that lineage from the other side: deploying Extreme in the field. Working through one of Brazil's first and best Extreme Networks partners, the work built metropolitan research networks for RNP, Brazil's National Education and Research Network.
extreme.s1Body: The project was the physical build-out of RNP's RedeComep program, Redes Comunitárias de Educação e Pesquisa, the initiative that gave Brazilian cities community-run metropolitan networks for education and research. Extreme nodes went in across Campinas and its region in São Paulo, the twin cities of Petrolina in Pernambuco and Juazeiro in Bahia, Marabá in Pará, and Castanhal in Pará. Each city's network was a long-distance metropolitan-area optical ring running EAPS, Extreme's Ethernet Automatic Protection Switching, the ring-resiliency protocol that keeps a fibre ring converged when a span fails, connecting RNP's point of presence to the federal and state higher-education and scientific-research institutions it served.
extreme.s2Body: Installing the nodes was only half of it. The other half was making the local teams self-sufficient: delivering ExtremeXOS (EXOS) Switching and Routing training, and the knowledge transfer that goes with it, how their systems had been configured, how the whole thing worked, and how to manage, troubleshoot, and expand it once the deployment team had moved on. Handing over the ability to run and grow the network was the point.
extreme.certs: Extreme Networks Certified Instructor. Extreme is one of the four platforms taught today; the current instructor authorizations are listed under Training. The work here, in 2013 to 2014, was field deployment and training delivery.

distribution.name: FireEye, McAfee and Ixia
distribution.tagline: A portfolio of security and test-and-measurement vendors, carried into the Brazilian channel through value-added distribution.
distribution.intro: A different kind of relationship from the rest of this list. For several years the work sat on the distribution side of the market, carrying vendors' technology into the Brazilian channel as a value-added distribution engineer, rather than working for any one of them directly. Three names from that portfolio are worth recording: FireEye, McAfee, and Ixia.
distribution.s1Body: Each covered a different corner of the market. FireEye was advanced threat detection, sandboxing and analysis aimed at the targeted, evasive attacks that signature-based tools miss. McAfee brought a broad endpoint- and network-security line. Ixia was the outlier and the interesting one: network test, measurement, and traffic generation, the equipment used to prove how networks and security devices behave under load, later acquired by Keysight. Together they spanned defence and the tools that verify it.
distribution.s2Body: Value-added distribution is its own skill. The role was pre-sales technical: architecting solutions, running demonstrations and proofs of concept, and enabling the resellers and integrators who took these products to end customers, translating each vendor's roadmap into something a partner could actually deploy and support. It is a vantage point that sees a lot of the market at once, across many vendors and many customer problems at the same time.
distribution.certs: Vendor and distribution enablement across the portfolio, rather than a single certification track.
